The average-reward criterion in an MDP is defined based on the gain of a policy, where the average reward per stage or the gain of the policy starting from a state is calculated.
The Cesaro limit is used to calculate the average reward and the value function of any stationary policy in an MDP.
A policy π⋆ is considered Blackwell-optimal if there exists a discount factor ¯γ such that the value function is maximized for all policies when γ ≥ ¯γ.
The optimal gain for a state is defined as the maximum average reward achievable by any policy, denoted by ρ⋆(s).
The Bellman equation describes the relationship between the optimal gain and the value function for each state in the MDP.

A weakly communicating MDP is defined by the partitioning of states into transient and reachable subsets, where all states in S1 are transient and any state in S2 can be reached from any other state under some stationary policy.
